answer:
**Charles Comte's strongest comparative claim** appears in the conclusion to *Traité de Législation* Vol. III (Ch. 37), where he synthesizes observations across continents, species (*espèces*: Caucasian, Mongol, Malay, American, Ethiopian), and climates: civilization originates in central (hot/temperate) equatorial regions due to abundant means of subsistence (plant/animal species thriving there), spreads poleward but halts at certain latitudes/elevations due to scarcity; polar/barbarian (hunter/pastoral) hordes perpetually invade centers, imposing uniform military aristocracies/slavery despite species differences—proving *local physical circumstances* (soil, heat, resources) dictate social organization/morals far more than innate racial traits.[^1] Similar governments/vices recur everywhere barbarism conquers industry (e.g., feudal-like regimes in Africa, Abyssinia, Malay archipelagos, pre-Columbian America), with no species exceptions.[^2]

**Charles Dunoyer's strongest comparative claim** is in his *Nouveau traité d'économie* Vol. I Introduction: human liberty—defined as free exercise of faculties via industry (skill overcoming nature) + morality (restraining violence)—increases monotonically across evolutionary "modes of existence" (*manières de vivre*): savage (hunting/war: minimal, via bare survival/independence); nomadic (pastoral: more, via herds/mobility); sedentary/slave-supported; serfdom; privilege/monopoly; office-seeking; culminating in *pure industry* (division of useful labor sans exploitation), where all professions (agriculture to governance) produce accumulating utilities in things/men, maximizing liberty.[^3] He reiterates in Vol. II Ch. 13: species progresses through these, with liberty proportional to culture (rejecting innate racial barriers).[^4]

**Methods**: Comte is more *empirical*, grounding claims in exhaustive traveler accounts (e.g., Bruce on Abyssinians, Cook on Tierra del Fuego/Van Diemen, Péron on New Holland), systematically comparing specific peoples/climates (e.g., Mongols cold vs. hot Asia: latter less barbarous), rejecting deductive racial superiority (e.g., Blumenbach/Lawrence cranial claims) due to contradictory facts/local variance.[^5] Dunoyer is more *deductive*: starts with axiomatic liberty=industry+morality (contra "rights" declamations), deduces progression across modes, selectively illustrates via history/travelers (e.g., Raynal/Rousseau critiqued), fitting facts to theory rather than inducting from raw data.[^6]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, Ch. 37 "Conclusion de ce livre" [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/37-conclusion-de-ce-livre/translation/#p-a4473d).

[^2]: Ibid., [Ch. 37](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/37-conclusion-de-ce-livre/translation/).

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Ch. 0 "INTRODUCTION" [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-d5b62b).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, Ch. 13 "III. Des divers ordres de travaux..." [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-c248a1).

[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, Ch. 47 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/47-parallele-entre-les-diverses-especes-d-hommes-de-l/translation/#p-696635); Ch. 48 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/48-de-la-superiorite-des-peuples-d-espece-caucasienne/translation/#p-ccdae1).

[^6]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Ch. 0 [paragraph](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-a373e4); Ch. 5 [full chapter](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/05-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-pe/translation/).
